title: Biodiversity Change Caused by Genetic Techniques
subtitle: Why one technique can help and harm life's variety at once
subject: Biology
syllabusDotPoints: evaluate the effect on biodiversity of using biotechnology in agriculture
inquiryQuestion: Does artificial manipulation of DNA have the potential to change populations forever?
lessonIntent: Students can evaluate the biodiversity effects of biotechnology separately at the genetic, species and ecosystem levels using balanced, conditional language.
examSkill: Evaluate a biotechnology's biodiversity effect by naming the level affected and stating whether diversity is increased, maintained or reduced.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Biology. Today, how genetic techniques change biodiversity. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: Biodiversity Change Caused by Genetic Techniques (Year 12 Biology, Module 6, Lesson 11)

scenes.heading: Can biotechnology reverse extinction?
scenes.body: By 2018 only two northern white rhinos were left, both female. The only path to offspring runs through banked cells.
scenes.callout: The same toolkit can rescue a species or quietly narrow its diversity.
scenes.caption: The northern white rhino case frames biotechnology's double-edged effect on biodiversity.

voiceover.text: By 2018, the northern white rhinoceros was functionally extinct in the wild, with only two females left alive, Najin and Fatu. The San Diego Zoo's Frozen Zoo, running since 1976, holds banked cells from more than ten thousand species. In 2021, Hildebrandt and colleagues described the only realistic path to new rhino offspring, making synthetic gametes from those banked skin cells. So here's the tension for today. The very same genetic techniques that might rescue a species can also, in farming, quietly narrow the variety of life. By the end you'll be able to judge those effects properly, at three different levels.

scenes.heading: Biodiversity lives at three levels
scenes.body: Biodiversity is not just a species count. You have to check genetic, species and ecosystem diversity separately.

bullets.text: Genetic level: the variety of alleles within a population or species.
Species level: the variety of species present in a habitat.
Ecosystem level: the variety of habitats, communities and interactions.

scenes.secondary: A technique can help one level while harming another, which is exactly why a single yes or no answer is too simple.
scenes.callout: Always ask which level, then ask increased, maintained, or reduced.
scenes.caption: Biodiversity operates at genetic, species and ecosystem levels, judged separately.

voiceover.text: First, a framework that stops you giving shallow answers. Biodiversity is not just the number of species in an area, it actually works at three levels, and you have to look at each one. The genetic level is the variety of alleles within a population, the raw variation a species has to draw on. The species level is the variety of different species sharing a habitat. And the ecosystem level is the variety of habitats, communities and the interactions between them. The reason this matters is that a single technique can push these in opposite directions, helping at one level while harming another. So instead of asking is this good or bad, you ask, which level is affected, and is diversity being increased, maintained, or reduced.

scenes.heading: The words you need

bullets.text: Biodiversity: the variety of life at genetic, species and ecosystem levels.
Genetic diversity: variation in alleles within a population or species.
Monoculture: large-scale growing of one genetically similar crop type.
Conservation genetics: using genetic tools to manage and protect populations.

scenes.callout: Monoculture narrows variation; conservation genetics tries to protect it.
scenes.caption: Biodiversity, genetic diversity, monoculture, conservation genetics.

voiceover.text: Let's pin down four terms. Biodiversity is the variety of life, measured at those genetic, species and ecosystem levels. Genetic diversity, more narrowly, is the variation in alleles within a population or species, and it's the one most people forget. Monoculture is the large-scale growing of a single genetically similar crop type, prized for efficiency but thin on variation. And conservation genetics is the use of genetic information and tools to help manage and protect populations, for example spotting inbreeding before it becomes fatal. Keep monoculture and conservation genetics in mind, because they're the negative and the positive faces of this topic.

scenes.heading: How biotech can reduce biodiversity
scenes.body: When a few successful varieties dominate, genetic diversity drops and the whole crop becomes fragile.

bullets.text: Reliance on a few elite varieties lowers genetic diversity in the crop.
Low variation means low resilience to a new disease or climate shift.
Effects can spill over to non-target species and interactions.

scenes.secondary: A crop built on one dominant genotype is efficient until a disease finds it, then the missing variation becomes a resilience problem, not a strength.
scenes.callout: High productivity is not the same thing as high biodiversity.
scenes.caption: Uniformity from a few dominant genotypes lowers genetic diversity and resilience.

voiceover.text: Now the negative side. The clearest way biotechnology can reduce biodiversity is by pushing uniformity. If farmers everywhere plant the same few high-performing varieties, the genetic diversity inside that crop falls, because rare alleles simply stop being grown. And low variation means low resilience. A crop built on one dominant genotype looks brilliantly efficient right up until a new disease or a climate shift comes along, and then there's no genetic backup to fall back on. The same uniformity can ripple outwards too, affecting non-target species and the ecological interactions around the field. This is the big exam point, raising productivity is not the same as raising biodiversity, and a strong answer never confuses the two.

diagram.beforeContent: Many alleles present, so some plants survive a new disease.
diagram.afterContent: One genotype dominates, so one disease can hit the whole crop.

scenes.heading: How biotech can support biodiversity
scenes.body: Genetic tools also work for conservation, finding at-risk populations and guiding breeding.

bullets.text: Genetic analysis identifies low-diversity or at-risk populations.
Breeding management can reduce inbreeding and protect rare lines.
Better genetic data leads to better conservation decisions.

scenes.secondary: Tools like the banked rhino cells preserve alleles that would otherwise vanish, even though any living population they restore would still start out small.
scenes.callout: The same technology that narrows a crop can rescue a wild population.
scenes.caption: Conservation genetics identifies at-risk populations and guides breeding to protect diversity.

voiceover.text: But biotechnology is not only a threat to biodiversity, and a balanced answer says so. Conservation genetics uses the very same kind of tools for protection. Genetic analysis can identify populations that are at risk or already low in diversity, which is information you simply cannot get by eye. Breeding programs can then use that data to reduce inbreeding and protect rare genetic lines. And better genetic data feeds into better conservation decisions overall. Think back to the rhino, those banked cells preserve alleles that would otherwise be gone forever. The honest caveat is that any living population restored from a handful of individuals still starts out with very low diversity, so even the rescue has a cost.

scenes.heading: Name the level affected
scenes.question: For each case, state which biodiversity level is mainly affected and whether diversity is increased, maintained or reduced: (a) a region's farms all switch to one elite wheat genotype; (b) conservation genetics rescues a threatened frog species from extinction; (c) the switch to that wheat changes which insects and birds live around the fields.
scenes.coachNote: For each, name the level first (genetic, species or ecosystem), then the direction.
steps: (a) One dominant genotype across the crop attacks variation within the species, so this is the genetic level, reduced.
(b) Saving a whole species from extinction acts on the number of species present, so this is the species level, maintained or increased.
(c) Changing which insects and birds live nearby alters communities and interactions, so this is the ecosystem level, altered.
Decision rule: identify the level (genetic, species, ecosystem) before stating the direction of change.
Answer: (a) genetic, reduced; (b) species, maintained; (c) ecosystem, altered.
scenes.caption: (a) genetic, reduced; (b) species, maintained; (c) ecosystem, altered.

voiceover.text: Let's practise the skill that earns marks, naming the level. Part a, every farm in a region switches to one elite wheat genotype. That attacks variation within the species, so it's the genetic level, and diversity is reduced. Part b, conservation genetics rescues a threatened frog species from extinction. That changes the number of species present, so it's the species level, and diversity is maintained, arguably increased compared with letting it die out. Part c, that same wheat switch changes which insects and birds live around the fields. That's about communities and interactions, so it's the ecosystem level, and the honest word is altered, because it could go either way. Notice the method, name the level first, then state the direction. That structure alone lifts a vague answer into a precise one.

scenes.heading: Three traps
scenes.body: Trap 1: thinking biodiversity just means the number of species. It also means genetic and ecosystem variety.
scenes.secondary: Trap 2: assuming more productivity means more biodiversity. A bumper monoculture can have less genetic diversity. Trap 3: giving an absolute verdict. Real outcomes are usually mixed, so use conditional language and name the level.
scenes.callout: Three levels, not one; productivity is not diversity; judge case by case.
scenes.caption: Biodiversity is three levels; productivity is not diversity; verdicts must be conditional.

voiceover.text: Three traps that cost marks every year. Trap one, thinking biodiversity just means the number of different species in an area. It does not, it also includes the genetic variety within a species and the variety of whole ecosystems, and losing genetic diversity can doom a species even while the species count still looks fine. Trap two, assuming that more productivity means more biodiversity. A record-breaking monoculture can actually carry less genetic diversity than the patchwork it replaced. Trap three, the slogan answer, biotechnology always helps, or biotechnology always harms. Real outcomes are mixed, so the strongest responses use conditional language, may increase or may reduce, and they always name which level of biodiversity they're talking about.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: A new genetically modified crop doubles yield, and within a decade it has replaced most traditional local varieties. A student writes: this clearly increases biodiversity because the crop is thriving. What is wrong with that, and what is the better evaluation?
answerSteps: The student has confused productivity with biodiversity, which is the classic trap.
Replacing many traditional varieties with one dominant variety reduces genetic diversity in the crop.
That lower variation reduces resilience to future disease or climate change.
A better answer names the level: genetic diversity is reduced even though yield rose.
Answer: the crop thriving raises productivity, not biodiversity; genetic diversity is reduced.
scenes.caption: A thriving high-yield monoculture raises productivity but reduces genetic diversity.

voiceover.text: Quick check. A new genetically modified crop doubles yield, and within ten years it has replaced most of the traditional local varieties. A student writes, this clearly increases biodiversity because the crop is thriving. What's wrong with that, and what's the better evaluation? Pause and reason it through. Right, here's the problem. The student has fallen straight into the productivity trap, treating a healthy, high-yielding crop as if that meant more biodiversity. But replacing many traditional varieties with one dominant variety reduces genetic diversity in the crop, and that lower variation reduces resilience to future disease or climate change. The better answer names the level explicitly, genetic diversity has been reduced, even though yield went up. Thriving is about productivity, not about variety.

scenes.heading: Lock it in
points: Biodiversity has three levels: genetic, species and ecosystem.
Biotech can reduce diversity through uniformity and monoculture.
Biotech can support diversity through conservation genetics.
Productivity is not biodiversity; never equate the two.
Strong evaluations name the level and use conditional language.
scenes.finalPrompt: Evaluating a biotech effect? Name the level, then say increased, maintained or reduced.
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: three levels, monoculture risk, conservation benefit, productivity trap, conditional language.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five things to take away. First, biodiversity operates at three levels, genetic, species and ecosystem, and you check each one. Second, biotechnology can reduce diversity by pushing uniformity, the monoculture problem. Third, it can also support diversity through conservation genetics. Fourth, productivity is never the same as biodiversity, so don't equate a big yield with more variety. And fifth, the strongest evaluations name the level affected and use conditional language. Whenever you evaluate a biotech effect, just name the level, then say increased, maintained, or reduced. See you next lesson.
